0: <title>Impact of energy flow optimization on the mitigation of environmental consequences and costs in greenhouse cucumber production </title>
Farshad Soheilifard 1 Morteza Taki 1 Rosalie van Zelm 2
Received: 13 May 2020 / Accepted: 11 October 2020 / Published online: 15 October 2020 Springer-Verlag GmbH Germany, part of Springer Nature 2020
1: Cucumber, as a major cultivated plant in Iran, takes up more than 80% of greenhouse production. So, improving the production efficiency can save energy, reduce the environmental consequences and production costs, and also create a lot of job opportu- nities. The present study aimed to investigate the impact of energy flow optimization on the environmental impacts and costs in greenhouse cucumber production by data envelopment analysis (DEA) and life cycle assessment (LCA) methods. For environ- mental assessment, human health damage, ecosystem damage, and resource scarcity were considered and analyzed in the form of 8, 12, and 2 impact categories, respectively, using the ReCiPe2016 (H) method on endpoint level. Required data were collected through questionnaire from 30 cucumber growers. The results of the current status of environmental impacts showed the major contribution of fuels (natural gas and diesel) in all three main impact categories. Based on DEA analysis, 19 greenhouses (63%) were identified as inefficient units with a mean efficiency score of 0.70 (TE = 0.7). The highest inefficiency was related to the manure (72%), electricity (71.7%), and fuel (59%), respectively. Electricity and manure showed the highest saving potential, while the highest contribution to total energy saving was related to fuel (87%). Environmental analysis of optimized greenhouses showed that efficient use of inputs can reduce all environmental impacts between 7 and 80%, which highlights the importance of efficient resource management in this study. Also, production costs were decreased by about 67 $ tonne (196 to 129 $) after the optimization of the units.
Keywords Data envelopment analysis . Life cycle assessment . Energy efficiency Energy flow . Optimization

3: The agricultural sector is one of the major contributors to greenhouse gases (GHGs) emissions with the share of almost 14% (Cooper et al. 2011; Soheili-Fard et al. 2018). Considering the population growth and rising food global de- mand, this sector plays an important role in the world. In the agricultural production section, greenhouse structures can be considered as an alternative to meet increased demands for

food (Taki and Yildizhan 2018). The area under greenhouse production in Iran increased by almost 100% from 2002 to 2007 (Omid et al. 2011) and it is projected to proceed 20,000 ha. Setting suitable policies for efficient allocating inputs in greenhouses, in which the different aspects such as energy use, environmental issues, and economic matters are considered, can cause to sustainable greenhouse production (Yildizhan and Taki 2019; Mostashari-Rad et al. 2019). Cucumber is a major cultivated plant in Iran. More than 80% of greenhouse production is taken up by greenhouse cucumber. So, efficient use of inputs can save energy, reduce the environmental consequences and production costs, and create a lot of job opportunities (Mohseni et al. 2018). For this purpose, knowing the present status of energy flow, environ- mental impacts, and greenhouse cucumber production costs is necessary. In this case, some methods were developed by the researchers. The life cycle assessment (LCA) approach was developed to estimate the environmental impacts of produc- tion systems in their whole life cycle (Schröder et al. 2003) such as greenhouses. By applying this approach, the efficien- cy of limited natural resources can be improved (Nikkhah et al. 2015; Zhang et al. 2016; Eady 2017; Kouchaki- Penchah et al. 2017). In agri-food production systems, many researchers applied this approach in their studies such as tea (Soheili-Fard et al. 2018; Cichorowski et al. 2015; Kouchaki- Penchah et al. 2017), tomato and cucumber (Khoshnevisan et al. 2014; Zarei et al. 2019; Wang et al. 2018; Martínez- Blanco et al. 2011), greenhouse flower (Russo et al. 2007), wheat (Taki et al. 2018), saffron (Khanali et al. 2017), apple (Naderi et al. 2020), pepper (Naderi et al. 2019), hazelnut (Mostashari-Rad et al. 2021), and potato (Bolandnazar et al. 2020). After understanding the present status, energy flows in each entity should be optimized. For this purpose, the data envelopment analysis (DEA) methodology that estimates the relative efficiency of some homogeneous production systems (Cooper 2004) can be applied. In this methodology, each entity is considered as a decision-making unit (DMU). This method was first used by Charnes et al. (1978). They used the theory of production frontiers developed by Farrell (1957). Production units, farms, greenhouses, etc. can be considered as DMU. In this methodology, DMU are investigated in terms of efficiency. The DMU can be efficient and inefficient, then DEA makes some suggestions for efficient use of inputs in inefficient entities to improve their efficiency (Paramesh et al. 2018). DEA methodology is identified as a popular tool for researchers, and recently, it is widely used in crop production systems such as chickpea (Elhami et al. 2016), rice (Chauhan et al. 2006; Nabavi-Pelesaraei et al. 2017), wheat (Khoshnevisan et al. 2013a), orange (Nabavi-Pelesaraei et al. 2014), and corn (Esfahani et al. 2017). LCA+DEA approach has been recently considered by re- searchers to evaluate the eco-efficiency of DMU (Iribarren

et al. 2014). In this hybrid approach, environmental burdens and energy efficiency are investigated. Masuda (2016) found nitrogen fertilizer as the hotspot for improving the eco- efficiency of wheat production using this hybrid approach. Mohammadi et al. (2015) investigated the technical efficiency of rice production in the north of Iran. They considered 82 rice paddy fields. The results showed that by efficient use of in- puts, environmental impacts reduced from 8 to 25% for spring and summer farms. Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2012) used LCA+ DEA for grape production in Spain to estimate the operational efficiency of each grape producer. Based on the results, by reduction of the inputs (more than 30%), environmental bur- dens will be decreased about 34%. Ullah et al. (2016) applied this combined approach for investigating the eco-efficiency of cotton production in Pakistan. Fisheries (Vázquez-Rowe et al. 2010) and dairy farms (Cecchini et al. 2018) are other produc- tion systems that were recently investigated by this method.

6: The recent investigations showed that this method (LCA+ DEA) can be used in the greenhouse structures to decrease the amount of energy used and also ranking the efficient and inefficient structures. So, the researchers can provide some comprehensive instructions for the farmers to increase the efficiency of resources, and consequently mitigate the envi- ronmental pollutions, as well as enhancing the final income. This evaluation should be used for some greenhouse struc- tures in some especial areas in Iran. Golashan city in Isfahan province is one of the main greenhouse area that should be evaluated in terms of the details of energy flow. So, the current study aimed to investigate the energy flow, environmental impacts, and costs of current cucumber production and opti- mize the input consumption, and consequently mitigate the environmental impacts and costs of greenhouse cucumber production in this area. The current study has used the recently updated ReCiPe method named as ReCiPe2016 (Huijbregts et al. 2017) for environmental impact assessment and given a prospective to all beneficiaries about the production cost pro- file after energy flow optimization.
7: In this study, 30 questionnaires were prepared to calculate the energy flow for greenhouse cucumber production in Golshan city, Isfahan province, Iran (Fig. 1).

The number of greenhouse structures in this region was more than 60, but among them, 30 units were selected because they have more accurate data than other farmers. The ques- tionnaires were completed by interviewing with the

greenhouse owners in this region. The amount of different inputs used in cucumber production was determined on aver- age for this area. The selected greenhouses were all pointed arch with a vertical sidewall (tunnel-shaped) and were equal in height (gutter height) and width. These greenhouses were also similar in cooling, heating, nutrition, etc. systems but were affected by different management practices. In the present study, the hybrid LCA+DEA approach was used for the optimization of energy flow and the environmen- tal consequences of greenhouse cucumber production in the study area. LCA+DEA method can be applied in three or five steps (Lozano et al. 2009; Iribarren et al. 2010; Vázquez- Rowe et al. 2010). The first and second steps are the same for both methods, including life cycle inventory (LCI) and life cycle impact assessment (LCIA). In the three-step approach, the final step is the calculation and interpretation of each DMU based on LCI and LCIA results obtained from DEA optimization. In the five-step approach, the operational eco- efficiency of each DMU is calculated through the CCR model, and then based on that, LCI values are estimated. Finally, LCIA is done based on estimated LCI values. The detailed description of LCA and DEA methodologies is presented in the following parts:

9: Based on the ISO 14040 (2006a) and ISO 14044 (2006b) standards, the LCA approach includes the following steps:
10: In the first step, the system boundary, functional unit (FU), and assumptions were determined based on the defined goal for this study. Since this study aimed to present a perspective about the potential reductions of environmental impacts (hu- man health, ecosystems, and resources) through optimization of energy flow, the functional unit was determined as 1 tonne which is understandable for greenhouse owners and other beneficiaries. Also, this FU is very suitable for the assessment of potential reductions in production costs. Figure 2 shows the system boundary for greenhouse cucumber production.
11: In this step, resource consumptions and output (produced cu- cumber) are quantified. Foreground data were obtained through questionnaire from 30 cucumber producers. Out of 30 studied
Fig. 2 System boundary for greenhouse cucumber production

greenhouses, 5 greenhouses used diesel as fuel instead of natu- ral gas. The inventory data related to the inputs used for cucum- ber production have been presented in Table 1. Background data were obtained from Ecoinvent 3.0 (Wernet et al. 2016) and Agri-footprint (Durlinger et al. 2014) databases.
12: LCIA as the third step of LCA shows the environmental impacts of the inputs and outputs per impact category (Nabavi-Pelesaraei et al. 2014; Mohseni et al. 2018). In

the present study, the ReCiPe2016 (H) method (Huijbregts et al. 2017) was used on the endpoint level. Environmental impacts were investigated in three damage categories including human health damage (disability-ad- justed life years, DALY), ecosystem damage (loss of spe- cies over a year, species year), and resource scarcity (sur- plus cost in $). 14: Interpretation is the final step in LCA studies in which all hotspots are identified in each impact category, and possible solutions are provided for mitigating the environmental impacts.
15: For DEA, CCR (Charnes, Cooper, and Rhodes) model sug- gested by Charnes et al. (1978) and BCC (Banker, Charnes, and Cooper) model suggested by Banker et al. (1984) were used. The CCR and BCC model approaches are based on the constant returns to scale (CRS) and variable returns to scale (VRS), respectively. These DEA models can be used in two forms as input-oriented and output-oriented. The present study focused on the reduction of inputs with the same level

PTE shows how the resources are used in a DMU. A low PTE indicates inefficient use of resources. Actually, PTE is the TE of the BCC model based on VRS. It means that a change in inputs causes a disproportionate change in outputs (Mousavi-Avval et al. 2011). In the BCC model, TE is decomposed into PTE for management of the factors and scale efficiency (Khoshnevisan et al. 2013b). 21: Based on the results obtained from ReCiPe2016 (H), natural gas and diesel fuel were identified as the hotspots in three main categories including human health damage, ecosystem damage, and resource scarcity in natural gas-based and diesel- based greenhouses, respectively. The location of the studied area is one of the best regions in Iran based on the solar energy receiving in a year (more than 2000 kWh m2 year-1. Electricity and N-based fertilizer were other major contribu- tors to total environmental impacts. So, it is extremely recom- mended to use solar collectors and photovoltaic panels to de- crease fuel and electricity consumption. Using solar energy (free and clean source of energy) can increase the net profit, reduce atmospheric emissions, and create sustainable employ- ment. Also, it can contribute to enhancing the culture of the region and encouraging the use of renewable energy sources. Figure 4 shows the contribution of inputs in each main cate- gory. In the present study, all impact categories were calculat- ed based on 1 tonne of cucumber production and obtained as 0.0057 DALY, 0.00573 species, and 635 US $, respectively. The hotspots in each category in gas-based and diesel-based greenhouses have been shown in Table 3. Yarn and pesticide
had the lowest contribution to environmental impacts in gas and diesel-based greenhouses, respectively.

Results obtained from this study were the same as those obtained by Khoshnevisan et al. (2014). The amount of natu- ral gas consumption per FU varied between around 136 and 9610 m³ which shows large variation between greenhouses. It can be realized that there is no specific instruction for the efficient use of natural gas in cucumber greenhouses of the study area. Also, using inefficient heating systems can be con- sidered as a reason for this problem. Improving heating sys- tems and monitoring greenhouse conditions can significantly reduce energy wastes. Despite high natural gas consumption, its contribution to total production costs is only 5%; it is be- cause of subsidy paid by the government which decreases farmer's motivation for efficient use of this input. In terms of electricity, high environmental impacts of this input can be attributed to its generation in thermal power plants, which has a contribution of around 93% to total generated electricity in Iran (Soheili-Fard et al. 2018). In this regard, using renew- able energy for electricity generation can be crucial for reduc- ing the environmental impacts. Also, a large variation of elec- tricity consumption between greenhouses shows lack of per- spective in this regard and also shows huge electricity wastes in cucumber production. Improving irrigation systems by ap- plying more efficient electric pumps can be considered as an alternative for efficient use of electricity. About N-based fer- tilizers, it had a high contribution to total impacts in the marine eutrophication impact category where this result was in agree- ment with Wang et al. (2018), who compared organic and conventional cucumber production from environmental point of view. In terms of fertilizers, soil analysis can be very helpful for energy and cost savings through estimating the real soil needs for fertilizer.

Based on the results, the average cost for efficient and ineffi- cient DMU was 73 $ and 196 $, respectively, while target values for inefficient units were obtained about 129 $. Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2012) reported cost saving as around 150 $ saving per 1 tonne of grape production which was in line with saving obtained for cucumber production system. 39: This study investigated the potential for the mitigation of envi- ronmental impacts and costs in cucumber production through DEA+LCA. Based on the results obtained from the environmen- tal analysis, fuel (natural gas and diesel), electricity, and N-based fertilizer were identified as environmental hotspots in all three main impact categories. It can be attributed to the lack of specific instruction for using these inputs in the study area, inefficient heating systems, non-renewable resources for power generation, and inefficient irrigation systems. DEA analysis identified 19 greenhouses (63%) as inefficient units with a mean efficiency score of 0.70. Results revealed that manure, electricity, and fuel were used inefficiently in cucumber greenhouses with the overusing of 72, 71.7, and 59%, respectively. Through DEA, 74 and 65.5% of electricity and manure can be saved, respective- ly. Considering total energy saving, fuel (natural gas and diesel) was identified as the most effective input with the contribution of 83% to total energy saving. Life cycle impact assessment of inefficient DMU after optimization showed that mitigation of environmental impacts varied between 7 and 80%, depending on the degree of the unit's inefficiency. In the other words, the units with lower efficiency score (TE) had a higher potential for environmental improvement and consequently economical prof- itability. In terms of cucumber production costs, it was decreased by about 67 $ tonne (196 to 129 $) after the optimization of inefficient units. Considering the environmental hotspots and energy-intensive inputs in cucumber greenhouses, their perfor- mance can be improved by applying suitable production tech- niques and equipment and efficiently allocating inputs, which can assure the mitigation of environmental burdens as well as high performance with low costs in cucumber production.
